site.title: Chinese Dialogue Evaluation Project
site.description: Bilingual showcase for a Chinese dialogue evaluation project, covering context, method, core cases, findings, and materials.

project.title: Reusable chinese dialogue benchmark from long conversations
project.oneLiner: This project turns high-value slices from real long Chinese conversations into reusable benchmark cases, then compares models on dialogue strategy with a shared rubric and a multi-judge sensitivity check.
project.targetAudience: For teams working on Chinese LLM training, evaluation, and conversational products
project.vision: Built on real long-conversation data, the method can grow into a fuller evaluation pipeline: automatic segmentation, candidate selection, short-form and long-form case construction, and taxonomy-based diagnosis.
project.personaText: The main differences across models are not surface phrasing, but whether they choose the right response mode for the user's state: how they acknowledge, when they push or stop, how they handle boundaries, and whether they preserve relational continuity. Judge preference also shifts rankings, so the more useful question is how well a model adapts its strategy across user states.

cards.title: Long-context Chinese dialogue is about interaction strategy, not wording alone
cards.body: The project focuses on strategic failures in ongoing dialogue rather than factual QA errors: missing implicit needs, choosing the wrong stance, pushing or closing too early, or losing relational continuity. These issues usually surface only in longer exchanges, not single-turn prompts.

cards.title: A small but high-signal benchmark distilled from real conversations
cards.body: Using a large pool of real long-form Chinese conversations, the project compresses candidate windows and selects 16 formal cases, organized into four user-state buckets. Evaluation uses a shared rubric and error framework to compare stable behavioral differences across models.

cards.title: Differences show up in response strategy and style switching
cards.body: The three models differ consistently in stance, explanatory tendency, boundaries, push intensity, and timing of closure. A multi-judge check also shows that rankings are sensitive to judge preference. The key question is not one overall winner, but whether a model can switch strategy for different user states.

Bucket 1.label: Seems to ask for advice, actually seeks confirmation

Bucket 2.label: Seems analytical, actually tests whether it is understood

Bucket 3.label: Signals are subtle and must be picked up proactively

Bucket 4.label: Has a view already; needs calibration and acknowledgment

modelSection.title: Overall performance and main differences across the three models
modelSection.figureAlt: Comparison of three models on high-context Chinese dialogue tasks

modelRanking.summary: Under the current ChatGPT judge setting, it is the most natural overall and most consistently follows a strong rhythm: acknowledge first, judge second, then nudge lightly.
modelRanking.watchout: Its weakness appears later in the conversation, where it can shift too early into problem-solving and sound slightly over-structured.

modelRanking.summary: Ranks second overall. It keeps cleaner boundaries, stays on topic, and is comparatively restrained.
modelRanking.watchout: Its main weakness is weaker follow-through: it often closes down too early to avoid mistakes, which limits dialogue development.

modelRanking.summary: Ranks third overall. It abstracts quickly, but is also the most likely to speak from an explanatory, top-down position.
modelRanking.watchout: It is more prone to conceptualization, over-interpretation, and lecture-like replies that drift away from dialogue.

modelProbe.title: Multi-Judge Sensitivity Check
modelProbe.lead: Even with a shared rubric and error framework, different judge models still show meaningfully different preferences on the same replies.

cards.title: ChatGPT Judge Preference
cards.detail: This judge tends to reward replies that are steady, accurate, and gently forward-moving. Responses that first receive the user, then judge, then move the conversation one step forward tend to score best.

cards.title: Claude Judge Preference
cards.detail: This judge tends to prefer clear boundaries, less task-taking, less operationalization, and less moralizing. Replies that stay on topic while remaining restrained usually do better here.

cards.title: Gemini Judge Preference
cards.detail: On B1, B2, and B4, this judge also tends to prefer less task-taking and less top-down explanation, often favoring Claude. But on lighter companionship cases like B3, it prefers looser, more natural companion-like replies, which can favor Gemini.

cards.title: Disagreement Strength
cards.detail: In the four-case sensitivity check, average responder spread is 1.54 and average judge spread is 1.25. The models differ in stable ways, but judge preference is also strong enough to change who ranks first.

modelProbe.bottomNote: The same high-context input can call for different response modes. All three models have recognizable default styles, but none switches style quickly enough yet.

caseSection.title: Eight core cases covering four recurring interaction patterns
caseSection.scoreSubtitle: Scores are aggregated automatically from evaluation metadata

patterns.name: B1 / Seems to ask for advice, actually seeks confirmation
patterns.desc: User appears to be asking "what to do", but really needs confirmation of their feelings and judgment.
patterns.ideal: Ideal Response: Confirmation instead of task-taking

patterns.name: B2 / Seems analytical, actually tests whether it is understood
patterns.desc: User appears to be reviewing and analyzing, but is actually checking "do you truly understand me".
patterns.ideal: Ideal Response: A feeling of being understood, not just analysis

patterns.name: B3 / Signals are subtle and must be picked up proactively
patterns.desc: User doesn't speak out directly, emotions and needs are buried deep, requiring the model to proactively sense them.
patterns.ideal: Ideal Response: Recognize low-visibility signals, avoid being oblivious or over-imagining

patterns.name: B4 / Has a view already, needs calibration and acknowledgment
patterns.desc: User already has a direction, doesn't need re-guiding, but needs more detailed calibration and acknowledgment.
patterns.ideal: Ideal Response: Calibration, acknowledgment, sense of boundary

insightSection.highlightsTitle: What matters most from this evaluation round

highlights.title: The public main results use ChatGPT as judge
highlights.detail: The 16 public benchmark results currently use ChatGPT as the main judge. Dialogue tasks do not have a single ground-truth answer, and different teams may define an ideal reply differently.

highlights.title: Ranking is clearly sensitive to judge choice
highlights.detail: In the four-case multi-judge check, B1, B2, and B4 are more often awarded to Claude by Gemini and Claude judges, while B3 is more often awarded to Gemini.

highlights.title: The bigger issue is switching response mode, not who wins overall
highlights.detail: More important than picking one overall winner is this: all three models have stable default styles, but none adapts its response strategy quickly enough to the user's state.

hypotheses.title: Opening
hypotheses.detail: At the start of a conversation, the model should first create enough safety and space for the user to keep talking. Fast acknowledgment and emotional understanding matter, but abstract explanation is usually too early.

hypotheses.title: Middle
hypotheses.detail: Later, strategy should branch based on user agency: users with a clear view need calibration and alignment, while users who are confused or less agentic need light structure and gentle forward movement.

hypotheses.title: Later Turns
hypotheses.detail: The best strategy for later turns still needs more validation. What is already clear is that many models can handle the opening, while the real gap often shows up later.

hypotheses.title: Next Expansion
hypotheses.detail: If the benchmark expands, emotional intensity is likely a better axis than raw case count. The goal is to test whether the same implicit need requires different handling at higher intensity.
